name: Mental Health Networks
description: An example template for studying how a personal network both supports a participant and adds stress while they manage their mental health. It uses separate name generators for supportive and difficult relationships, and records who knows about the participant's mental health (disclosure).

items.content:
## For researchers

_Delete this screen before you field the study. It lists the instruments and studies this template draws on._

- [Kessler RC et al. The K6 psychological distress scale (overview, Cognitive Atlas).](https://www.cognitiveatlas.org/task/id/tsk_OA90UJX5qwTyc/)
- [Offer S, Fischer CS (2018). They Drive Me Crazy: Difficult Social Ties and Subjective Well-Being (UCNets). *J Health Soc Behav*.](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9242844/)
- [GSS "important matters" generator — Contributions of the GSS to Egocentric Network Research (TR45).](https://gss.norc.org/content/dam/gss/get-documentation/pdf/reports/topical-reports/TR45%20Egocentric%20Network.pdf)

## Thank you for taking part

In this interview we will talk about the people in your life and how they relate to your mental health and wellbeing. We will ask you to name people who **support** you, and also people who are sometimes a **source of stress, conflict, or strain**. There are no right or wrong answers — we are simply interested in your own experience.

### Some of these questions are personal

We will ask about how you have been feeling recently and about who knows about your mental health. You are free to skip any question, to pause, or to stop the interview at any time, and you do not have to give a reason. The people you name are recorded only by the first name or nickname you choose, and your answers are kept confidential and used only for research.

### Your consent

By continuing, you confirm that the researcher has explained the study to you, that you have had the chance to ask questions, and that you agree to take part. If you would like to stop at any point, just let the interviewer know.

**If talking about these topics brings up difficult feelings, please tell the interviewer — support is available, and there is a list of services at the end of the interview.**

introductionPanel.title: A few questions about you
introductionPanel.text: Before we talk about the people in your life, we would like to ask how you have been feeling **over the last 30 days**. Please answer as honestly as you can. Remember you can skip any question you would prefer not to answer.

fields.prompt: During the **last 30 days**, about how often did you feel nervous?
During the **last 30 days**, about how often did you feel hopeless?
During the **last 30 days**, about how often did you feel restless or fidgety?
During the **last 30 days**, about how often did you feel so depressed that nothing could cheer you up?
During the **last 30 days**, about how often did you feel that everything was an effort?
During the **last 30 days**, about how often did you feel worthless?
In general, how would you rate your mental health these days?
Are you currently receiving any treatment or support for your mental health?
fields.hint: For example, from a doctor, therapist, counsellor, or support service.

stages.label: People who support you
stages.interviewScript: This generator elicits supportive ties. Encourage the participant to think broadly — these can be people they confide in, turn to for advice, or rely on for practical help. The same person may also be named later as a source of stress; that is expected.

fields.prompt: What is this person's first name or a nickname you will recognise?

prompts.text: Who are the people you can **talk to about how you're feeling**, or confide in about personal matters?
Who do you turn to for **advice or guidance** when you have an important decision to make about your health or your life?
Who would you ask for **practical help** — for example if you were unwell and needed help for a couple of weeks?

stages.label: People who are difficult or draining
stages.interviewScript: This generator elicits difficult or draining ties (negative and ambivalent relationships). Reassure the participant that almost everyone has people like this, and that naming someone here does not mean the relationship is all bad. If a person they already named as supportive is also a source of stress, they can add them again here — this captures ambivalent ties.

fields.prompt: What is this person's first name or a nickname you will recognise?

prompts.text: Thinking about the people in your life, who do you **sometimes find demanding or difficult**, or who are a source of stress or conflict for you?

Who tends to **drain your energy** or leave you feeling worse after spending time with them? (If you have already named them as someone who supports you, drag them in from the 'Already mentioned' panel rather than typing their name again — this keeps them as a single person who is both supportive and difficult.)

introductionPanel.title: Tell us a little about each person
introductionPanel.text: For each person you named, we would like to understand your relationship with them, what kinds of support they provide, whether they know about your mental health, and how often you are in contact. There are no right answers — just describe things as they are for you.

fields.prompt: How is this person related to you?

fields.hint: Someone you can talk to about how you feel.

fields.prompt: Do they help you with **practical things**?
fields.hint: Such as tasks, money, or getting around.

fields.prompt: Do they give you **information or advice**?
fields.hint: Advice that helps you cope or make decisions.

fields.prompt: Does this person **know about your mental health**?
fields.hint: That is, have you told them about it?

fields.prompt: Is this person a **formal or clinical source of help**?
fields.hint: For example, a doctor, therapist, counsellor, or other professional.

fields.prompt: Is this person sometimes a **source of stress or conflict** for you?

stages.label: How close, and how often you're in touch

prompts.text: Drag each person into the band that best describes **how close you feel to them**.
Now drag the same people into the band that best describes **how often you are usually in contact** with them.
Sort the people you named by the kind of help they represent: **informal** help (family, friends, peers) or **formal/clinical** help (professionals and services).

stages.label: Arrange your network
stages.interviewScript: This is a reflective, manual-positioning stage (no automatic layout): invite the participant to arrange the people meaningfully — for example supportive people on one side, difficult or draining people on the other, and those they feel closest to nearer the centre of the rings — then draw lines between people who know one another. The arrangement they build becomes the network map they talk through on the final review screen, so the positions are meaningful here rather than incidental.

prompts.text: Here are the people you named. Take a moment to **arrange them in a way that feels right to you** — for example, placing the people who **support you** on one side and those who are **difficult or draining** on the other, and the people you feel **closest to** nearer the centre. Then draw a line between any **two people who know one another**.

stages.label: Your network of support and strain

presets.label: Who knows about your mental health

stages.label: Thank you and support services
stages.title: Thank you — and where to find support

items.content:
## Thank you for sharing

Talking about your mental health and your relationships takes courage, and we are grateful for your time and honesty.

### If you would like support

If any of these questions brought up difficult feelings, please consider reaching out for support:

- **Talk to the interviewer now** if you would like to stop and take a moment.
- **Contact your doctor or usual mental-health service.**
- **If you are in crisis or thinking about harming yourself, please contact your local emergency services or a crisis line immediately.** _The researcher will insert the specific local and national helpline numbers for your study site here (for example, in the US: call or text 988; in the UK: Samaritans on 116 123)._

You do not have to face difficult feelings alone, and reaching out for help is a sign of strength.
